notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s how these usually stack up for someone in your situation, and what’s worth checking for each.

Big picture priority for you:

Disability insurance
Renters insurance (with liability)
Umbrella insurance
Life insurance

(With health insurance actually being the true #1, even though you didn’t list it.)

1. Disability insurance (often the biggest gap)

Why it’s high priority:
Your biggest asset is your future income. Disability insurance is what protects that if you can’t work because of illness or injury. For a high-paying tech job, the lost income can be huge.

What to check at Microsoft (or similar large tech):

Short-term disability (STD):

Usually covers part of your income for the first few weeks/months (often up to 3–6 months).
Check:
What % of salary is covered (50%, 60%, 100%?).
How long it pays.
The waiting period (for example, 7 days).

Long-term disability (LTD):

This is the critical one.
Look at:
Benefit percentage: Many plans cover around 60% of base salary. Some exclude bonus/RSUs.
Definition of disability: “Own occupation” (can’t do your specific job) is stronger than “any occupation.”
Benefit duration: To age 65/67 is ideal; some only pay for a few years.
Who pays the premium:
If employer pays, benefits are usually taxable.
If you pay with after-tax dollars, benefits are usually tax-free, which is more valuable.

Typical priority for you:
Most people in your spot make sure they have solid LTD coverage (ideally 60%+ of income, own-occupation, to retirement age). If work coverage is weak, some add an individual policy to fill the gap.

2. Renters insurance (with liability)

Why it matters for a renter:

Protects your stuff (electronics, furniture, clothes, etc.) from things like theft, fire, and some water damage (within policy limits).
Includes personal liability if someone gets hurt in your place or you accidentally damage someone else’s property (like a kitchen fire spreading).

What to look for:

Personal property coverage amount:

Roughly total up what it would cost to replace everything you own.
Choose between:
Replacement cost (pays what it costs to buy new today – usually preferred).
Actual cash value (pays depreciated value – cheaper but weaker).

Liability coverage:

Many people go for at least $300K–$500K in personal liability.
Check if it includes legal defense costs.

Loss of use:

Pays for temporary housing if your place becomes uninhabitable (fire, etc.).

Deductible:

Higher deductible = lower premium, but more out-of-pocket per claim.

Typical priority for you:
It’s usually pretty cheap and covers both your belongings and liability risk. Many single renters treat this as a must-have once they own more than a few thousand dollars of stuff.

3. Umbrella insurance

What it is:
Extra liability coverage that sits on top of your auto and renters policies (for example, an extra $1M+ of protection).

Why people get it:
To protect current assets and future earnings if they’re sued for a large amount (serious car accident, someone badly injured at your place, etc.).

For someone like you:

Pros:

Can be relatively cheap for $1M of coverage.
Helpful if you:
Drive a lot.
Have higher income and future earnings to protect.
Host gatherings, have a dog, or other risk factors.

Cons / why it’s often later:

Usually more important once you have significant assets (high net worth, home equity, large investments).
Requires certain minimum liability limits on auto and renters first (for example, $250K/$500K auto, $300K renters).

Typical priority for you:
Often nice-to-have but not first-line for a single renter with modest assets. Many people revisit umbrella coverage as their net worth and income grow.

4. Life insurance

Purpose:
To provide for people who depend on your income if you die (partner, kids, aging parents you support, etc.).

For a single person with no dependents:

Often low priority beyond:

Any free basic life insurance from your employer (for example, 1x salary).
Enough to cover final expenses and maybe small debts if that matters to you.

You might consider more if:

You have co-signed debts (for example, with parents).
You expect to have dependents soon and want to lock in insurability while you’re healthy.

Typical priority for you:
A lot of people in your situation just keep the employer-provided life insurance and don’t buy extra until they have dependents or shared financial obligations.

How to use this

For a single renter at a large tech company, a common priority order to review is:

Disability insurance: Confirm strong long-term disability coverage through work; ask yourself if 60% of income (taxable or not) would actually cover your lifestyle if you couldn’t work.
Renters insurance with liability: Make sure you have it, with enough personal property coverage and at least a few hundred thousand in liability.
Umbrella insurance: Consider once your income and assets are higher or if you have bigger liability risks.
Life insurance: Usually minimal need beyond employer coverage unless someone depends on your income now or will soon.

If you want to go deeper, you can share what you currently have (disability details, renters limits, any life insurance), and Monarch can help you spot specific gaps and what to adjust next.
